title: Attach the MCP Server to a Foundry Agent
summary: Microsoft Foundry Agent Service supports connecting agents to remote MCP server endpoints. The agent receives a server label, server URL, optional project connection for authentication, and approval policy.
analogy: Attaching an MCP server is like adding a specialized radio channel to a command center. The commander can request information or actions over that channel, but the channel still has call signs, permissions, and approval rules.
diagram:
graph TD
Project[Foundry project] --> Agent[Agent]
Agent --> MCPTool[MCP tool attachment]
MCPTool --> Server[Incident MCP server]
Server --> Backend[Incident backend]
body:
## First principles

First principles: the model should not know how to authenticate to every backend. Foundry hosts the agent, attaches an MCP endpoint as a tool, and mediates tool approval and execution.

## Definition

Microsoft Foundry Agent Service supports connecting agents to remote MCP server endpoints. The agent receives a server label, server URL, optional project connection for authentication, and approval policy. The agent can then discover and call MCP tools through Foundry.

## How it fits together

Use the generic MCP attach pattern: create an AIProjectClient with DefaultAzureCredential, define an MCPTool with server_label and server_url, require approval for risky calls, and add the tool to the agent definition. The incident server URL is representative until your organization provides the real endpoint.

## Worked example

```python
# Representative Foundry SDK wiring. The MCP mechanics are documented by Microsoft;
# the incident server URL and tool names are representative.
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition, MCPTool

PROJECT_ENDPOINT = "https://YOUR-RESOURCE.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/YOUR-PROJECT"
INCIDENT_MCP_URL = "https://incident-mcp.contoso.internal/mcp"  # representative

project = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=PROJECT_ENDPOINT,
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),  # managed identity when running in Azure
)

incident_mcp = MCPTool(
    server_label="incident-management",
    server_url=INCIDENT_MCP_URL,
    require_approval="always",  # safest default for incident write-capable tools
    # project_connection_id may be used when your Foundry project stores the MCP auth setup.
)

agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name="IncidentHelper",
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model="gpt-5-mini",
        instructions="Help responders read incidents. Ask approval before writes.",
        tools=[incident_mcp],
    ),
)
print(agent.name, agent.version)
```

*Representative Foundry agent with an incident MCP tool*

drills.q: What are the two key MCP attachment fields?
Why set require_approval to always in this lane?
drills.a: Incident tools can change operational records, so human approval should be the default.

drills.q: What credential object is used by the Foundry client?

teachItBack: Foundry attaches an MCP server to an agent as a tool. The agent can then discover and call server tools through Foundry's orchestration path. For incident systems, attach with managed identity and require approval for write actions.

glossary.definition: Python SDK client for a Foundry project endpoint.
Foundry SDK model describing a remote MCP server attached to an agent.
Configuration controlling whether tool calls require human confirmation.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Connect to MCP Server Endpoints for agents
Microsoft Learn: Set Up MCP Server Authentication

quiz.q: What does the token audience identify in managed-identity auth?
choices: The intended receiving service for the access token
The human reader of the final answer
The number of available MCP tools
quiz.explanation: The audience tells Entra and the receiver which service the token is meant for.
